project_description.describe_what_work_needs_to_be_done:
Our primary need is significant repair and partial replacement of the church’s original 1954 education wing roof and associated water-damaged structural elements. Over the past several years, we have experienced increasing roof leaks during moderate to heavy rains, particularly over the children’s classrooms on the second floor and the hallway leading to the fellowship hall. During two separate storms this past spring, ceiling tiles collapsed in Classroom 3B and the upstairs hallway, and water ran down the interior walls into the first-floor nursery and storage rooms.

Specifically, the work that needs to be done includes:

1. Roof Replacement and Structural Repair
- Remove the existing aging asphalt shingle roof over the two-story education wing (approximately 4,200 square feet).
- Inspect and replace any rotted or compromised roof decking and underlying structural members, particularly near the northeast corner where repeated leaks have occurred.
- Replace deteriorated flashing around the chimney, roof vents, and where the education wing roof ties into the main sanctuary roof.
- Install new underlayment, ice and water shield in vulnerable areas, and high-quality architectural shingles rated for at least 30 years.
- Install proper ridge venting and additional intake vents to improve attic ventilation and reduce moisture buildup.

2. Interior Ceiling and Wall Repair
- Remove and replace all stained, sagging, or mold-affected ceiling tiles in the second-floor hallway and Classrooms 3A, 3B, and 3C (approximately 650 square feet of ceiling surface).
- Cut out and replace damaged sections of drywall in the upstairs hallway and in the first-floor nursery where water has run down the walls. Several areas currently have bubbling paint, soft drywall, and visible water lines.
- Treat any exposed framing or surfaces that have been repeatedly wet with an appropriate mold-resistant primer and sealant, as recommended by the contractor.
- Repaint ceilings and walls in the affected hallways and classrooms to provide a uniform appearance and prevent future peeling.

3. Gutter and Downspout Improvements
- Remove and dispose of the existing narrow, rusted gutters along the east and north sides of the education wing.
- Install appropriately sized seamless gutters and downspouts designed to handle heavy Midwest rainfalls.
- Add two additional downspouts and redirect water flow away from the foundation, including splash blocks or extensions to move water at least 6 feet from the building.

4. Safety and Accessibility Considerations
- Ensure that all work is performed in a way that allows continued use of at least part of the education wing during construction, as this is where our children’s Sunday School and midweek youth programs are held.
- Temporarily relocate classrooms while work is being done, and once complete, ensure that lighting fixtures and ceiling-mounted smoke detectors are properly reinstalled.

The main concern driving this request is the safety of our children and older adults who use this section of the building almost every day of the week. In recent months, we have had to place buckets in the hallway during storms to catch dripping water, and we have had at least two instances where children commented on “the smell” in Classroom 3B after a rain, which we fear might be early mold growth behind the walls or above the ceiling.

Our Board of Trustees has done what it can with volunteer labor to patch and re-seal portions of the roof over the past five years, but those small repairs no longer hold for more than one or two storms, and the underlying decking has now been compromised in several spots. The building is heavily used for community outreach (including our weekday after-school tutoring program and a weekly NA group that meets in the education wing), so simply closing off the space is not a realistic option. We are seeking assistance to complete this work properly and in a way that will be sustainable for the next few decades, rather than continuing to patch and re-patch as more leaks appear.
project_description.what_was_communicated_in_professional_ev:
Yes. In August 2025, our Board of Trustees invited two local roofing and construction companies to inspect the education wing and provide written estimates.

1. Ozark Faith Roofing & Construction (Springfield, MO)
- Date of inspection: 2025-08-19
- The inspector, Mark Ellison, spent approximately 2.5 hours on-site. He inspected both the exterior roof and the interior spaces under the affected areas.
- Findings:
  • The existing shingles are at the end of their serviceable life, with widespread granule loss, curling, and multiple patched areas.
  • Several sections of roof decking, particularly near the northeast corner and along the east gutter line, show clear signs of long-term water intrusion. He probed the decking from the attic and found soft spots and discoloration.
  • The flashing where the education wing roof meets the main sanctuary roof was improperly layered, likely from a repair done decades ago, and is allowing wind-driven rain to penetrate.
  • Interior ceiling tiles in the second-floor hallway and Classrooms 3A–3C are stained and some are sagging, indicating repeated wetting. He noted a musty odor in Classroom 3B and recommended cautious handling and proper drying/treatment but did not see widespread visible mold.
- Recommendations:
  • Full tear-off and replacement of the education wing roof (approximately 4,200 sq. ft.), including replacement of any rotten decking, new underlayment, ice and water shield in valleys and along eaves, and new 30-year architectural shingles.
  • Rework and properly flash the roof-to-wall and roof-to-roof transitions, particularly at the joint with the sanctuary roof.
  • Replace existing gutters with properly sized seamless aluminum gutters and add additional downspouts to handle heavy rainfall and reduce overflow.
  • Remove and replace damaged ceiling tiles and any drywall that shows clear signs of water damage, then prime with a mold-resistant primer and repaint.
- Cost estimate from Ozark Faith Roofing & Construction:
  • Roof tear-off and replacement (including decking repairs allowance): $42,500
  • Gutter and downspout replacement and reconfiguration: $4,800
  • Interior repairs to ceilings and walls (materials and labor): $7,200
  • Total estimated cost (before any unforeseen structural discoveries): $54,500

2. Heartland Church Builders, LLC (Nixa, MO)
- Date of inspection: 2025-09-02
- Their project manager, Sarah Kim, conducted a walkthrough with our Trustees Chair and Pastor.
- Findings:
  • Confirmed much of what the first contractor reported, including multiple soft spots in the decking and improperly installed flashing.
  • Noted that the attic ventilation is inadequate, causing heat buildup and potentially shortening the life of any new shingles unless additional ridge and intake vents are installed.
  • Observed that the existing gutters are undersized and have poor pitch, leading to water spilling over and running down the exterior walls during heavy rains.
- Recommendations:
  • Full tear-off of the existing roof system and installation of a new roofing system with upgraded underlayment and ventilation.
  • Replacement of all flashing and counter-flashing around penetrations and where the roofs intersect.
  • Installation of new, larger-capacity seamless gutters with correctly pitched downspouts, including two additional downspouts on the east side.
  • Coordination with a drywall/paint subcontractor to repair and repaint damaged interior areas.
- Cost estimate from Heartland Church Builders, LLC:
  • Roof replacement and ventilation improvements: $45,300
  • Gutter and downspout system: $5,100
  • Interior ceiling and wall repairs: $6,600
  • Total estimated cost: $57,000

Both contractors emphasized that the damage, while not catastrophic yet, will worsen if addressed only with temporary patches. They also advised that delaying the work by another year or two could result in significantly higher costs if more of the roof decking and structural framing become compromised. We have attached copies of both written bids and the itemized estimates from each contractor to this application, as requested. Our Board of Trustees has reviewed these estimates and is using the lower estimate of $54,500 as our working budget figure, while recognizing that unforeseen issues could push the final cost closer to the higher estimate.

financial_capacity_and_fundraising.briefly_tell_why_the_congregation_cannot:
Grace Harbor United Methodist Church is a mid-sized, aging congregation with limited financial flexibility. Our total operating budget for the current calendar year (2025) is $176,000, which covers pastoral salary and benefits, part-time staff (administrative assistant, music director, and nursery coordinator), utilities, insurance, program ministries, and routine building maintenance. Over the past three years, our giving has remained relatively flat while our fixed costs—particularly insurance, utilities, and health benefits—have steadily increased.

Several factors contribute to our inability to undertake this project solely with our own resources:

1. Aging Congregation and Limited Income Growth
A significant portion of our active membership is on fixed incomes. We estimate that roughly 55–60% of our regular attenders are retired, including many who live on Social Security and modest pensions. While they are generous with what they have, they simply do not have the capacity to dramatically increase their giving. We have already conducted a stewardship emphasis in 2024 and again in early 2025 encouraging sacrificial giving, and while we saw a small uptick, it was not enough to create the kind of surplus needed for a $50,000–$60,000 capital project.

2. Prior Capital Commitments and Emergency Repairs
In 2022, we had to replace the original boiler that heated both the sanctuary and the education wing after it failed in early January. That project cost approximately $38,000, and we had to draw down most of our reserves and take a small internal loan from our Memorial Fund to complete it. In 2023, we also incurred an unexpected $9,500 expense to bring our fire alarm system into compliance with updated codes after an inspection by the fire marshal. These back-to-back, unplanned capital expenses depleted our savings and left us with a very modest emergency reserve of around $12,000 as of the start of 2025.

3. Limited Current Reserves and Cash Flow Constraints
Our current unrestricted reserves (as of October 2025) are approximately $15,400. Our monthly operating expenses average between $13,800 and $14,500, leaving very little margin for additional commitments without jeopardizing our ability to pay staff and keep up with utilities and insurance. Even if we were to dedicate every available dollar of surplus for the next year, we would still fall far short of the estimated $54,500–$57,000 needed for this roof and interior repair project.

4. Commitment to Mission and Community Ministry
We host a number of community ministries that, while not revenue-generating, are central to our identity and calling as a church. These include:
- A weekday after-school tutoring program for elementary students from the nearby West Ridge Apartments, most of whom are from low-income families.
- A weekly Narcotics Anonymous (NA) group that meets in our education wing on Tuesday evenings.
- Monthly community meals that serve 60–80 neighbors, many of whom depend on this meal as a key part of their food security.

Cutting back significantly on these ministries to fund the roof project would undermine our mission and damage relationships with the families and neighbors who rely on us. We have already trimmed our program budgets as much as we feel we can without hollowing out our ministry.

5. Difficulty Securing Traditional Financing
We did explore the possibility of a bank loan or line of credit. However, our local bank indicated that, given our limited reserves and highly constrained cash flow, any loan large enough to cover the project would likely require payments that we could not reliably sustain without severe cuts elsewhere. The conference treasurer’s office also advised caution about taking on additional debt at this time, given our already-tight operating budget.

For all of these reasons, while we are committed to contributing what we reasonably can (both financially and via volunteer labor), we simply cannot bear the full cost of this project on our own without compromising the basic stability of the church’s finances and undermining our core ministries. Assistance from the West District would allow us to address a serious and growing safety issue in a responsible way, while still maintaining our ongoing commitments to worship, discipleship, and community outreach.
financial_capacity_and_fundraising.if_so_describe_fundraising_activities:
As of November 2025, the congregation has set aside $8,750 specifically for this roof and repair project. This amount has come from a combination of designated gifts, a small transfer approved by the Church Council from our Memorial Fund (for building preservation), and proceeds from two modest fundraising efforts held earlier this year (a spring rummage sale and a fall chili supper).

Recognizing that these funds cover only a fraction of the estimated cost, we have planned several additional fundraising efforts for the next 9–12 months, with the understanding that every dollar we can raise locally will reduce the amount we need to request from the district. Our planned activities include:

1. “Raise the Roof” Capital Campaign (January–June 2026)
- A six-month focused giving campaign, introduced with a sermon series and personal testimonies from families whose children use the education wing.
- Members and regular attenders will be invited to make one-time gifts or 6–12 month pledges designated for the roof project.
- We will provide simple pledge cards and monthly updates in worship and via email, highlighting progress and sharing stories about why the education wing is important for our ministries (children’s Sunday School, youth group, after-school tutoring, etc.).
- Our conservative hope is to raise an additional $10,000–$15,000 through this focused internal appeal, based on preliminary conversations with several key families and leaders.

2. Community Benefit Concert and Dessert Auction (Spring 2026)
- Our music director is organizing an evening concert featuring our church choir, the youth praise band, and two local musicians who have already agreed to participate.
- During intermission, we will hold a dessert auction, with homemade pies, cakes, and cookies donated by church members and friends. In the past, similar dessert auctions for mission trips have raised $1,500–$2,000 in a single evening.
- We plan to widely advertise the event in the neighborhood and through social media, emphasizing that all proceeds will go directly to the education wing roof and safety repairs.

3. Grant Exploration and Matching Gifts
- A small team from the Board of Trustees and Church Council has begun researching possible small grants or matching programs offered by local foundations, community organizations, or businesses for building improvements that benefit children and community programs.
- While we do not have any commitments yet, we are preparing a basic grant narrative and hope to submit at least two or three applications in early 2026. If any of these are successful, we intend to apply them directly to this project.

4. Ongoing Smaller Efforts
- We will continue to direct proceeds from our annual fall rummage sale and spring plant sale toward the roof fund for at least the next two years. These events typically raise between $1,000 and $1,500 each.
- Several members have already indicated they plan to designate part of their annual required minimum distributions (RMDs) from retirement accounts as Qualified Charitable Distributions specifically for the roof project in 2026.

In addition to these monetary efforts, many members have pledged to support the project through volunteer labor where appropriate (e.g., demolition of interior ceiling tiles, painting, cleanup) and by providing meals and hospitality for any work teams that come to assist. While these contributions do not reduce the contractor’s bill directly, they will significantly reduce overall project-related expenses and foster stronger congregational ownership of the repairs.
